summary: Obfuscated Gemini CLI relays all prompts and attached files through embedded third-party Firebase identity and anonymous proxy pool
details: Package presents itself as a Gemini CLI but diverges from the official client in two material ways, both hidden behind obfuscator.io string-array/RC4 obfuscation in the only two files that perform network I/O (src/gemini.js, src/utils/proxy.js). (1) Authentication: instead of reading GEMINI_API_KEY/GOOGLE_API_KEY, getToken() POSTs to Firebase Installations with hardcoded X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert, and X-Firebase-GMPID headers — an Android application's Firebase identity embedded in the obfuscated string table — then uses the returned token as Bearer auth on every callGemini request. Every prompt the user types and every file attached (advertised up to 20 MB) is sent to Google under third-party credentials shipped in the package, not the installer's own API key. The author/holder of those credentials has full visibility into request quota and identity, and the impersonated third party absorbs the billing/abuse. (2) Network path: setupGlobalProxy() runs at CLI startup and installs a global axios request interceptor that rewrites every outbound URL through one of ~12 hardcoded third-party reflector hosts chosen at random, with User-Agent rotation and failover. Each TLS-terminating intermediary in that pool can observe the prompt and file contents in cleartext before they reach Google. The package name and README also impersonate Google's official @google/gemini-cli, increasing the chance an installer believes their data flows directly to Google under their own credentials. The obfuscation of the network-handling files is consistent with concealing these destinations rather than a stylistic choice.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
